email_title: CONSISTBENCH: A Cross-Format, Cross-Phrasing Consistency Benchmark for Large Language Models

sections.summary: This paper introduces CONSISTBENCH, a benchmark designed to evaluate LLM consistency along two orthogonal axes: cross-format (MCQ, open-ended, yes/no, true/false, fill-in-the-blank) and cross-phrasing (six paraphrase types) with an additional domain-stratified analysis across six knowledge areas. It proposes a suite of consistency metrics (CFA, CPA, PFI, FCAG, DCS) that decouple consistency from accuracy and reports empirical findings on six open-weight models (3.8B–70B), showing large cross-format inconsistency (CFA ~56–63%), pronounced fragility to negation-framing paraphrases, and sizable domain variation. The authors validate their pipeline with a two-stage answer-equivalence system (rule-based + LLM judge) and perform sensitivity analyses that show the core findings persist even when restricting to constrained formats that do not require an LLM judge.
sections.strengths:
Technical novelty and innovation
The joint evaluation of cross-format and paraphrase-type-stratified consistency at the item level is new and practically relevant; prior work typically studies either format conversion or paraphrase robustness in isolation.
The metric suite (CFA, CPA, PFI, FCAG, DCS) is well-motivated to separate consistency from accuracy and enables a multi-faceted diagnosis.
The explicit typology of paraphrase categories, with controlled negation framing, reveals a sharp and actionable pattern (negation vs. non-negation) across models.


Experimental rigor and validation
The sensitivity analysis that separates constrained pairs (string-match only) from open-involving pairs helps bound the effect of LLM-judge errors and preserves the headline conclusion (CFA far from 100%).
Sound statistical reporting (bootstrap CIs, McNemar tests with Bonferroni corrections, ANOVA/Kruskal-Wallis for domain effects); consistency–accuracy decoupling analysis is clearly presented.
Scaling and domain breakdowns provide deeper insights beyond aggregate metrics.


Clarity of presentation
The paper is generally well-written, with clear definitions of metrics and transparent acknowledgment of limitations.
Figures and tables are informative and align with the major claims; the narrative emphasizes item-level agreement rather than only aggregate accuracy.


Significance of contributions
Consistency is central to reliable deployment; diagnosing cross-format and paraphrase sensitivity fills an important gap left by accuracy-centric benchmarks.
The identification of negation as uniquely destabilizing is convergent with broader literature and practically valuable for mitigation and prompting strategies.
sections.weaknesses:
Technical limitations or concerns
Heavy reliance on an LLM-as-judge with only 72% agreement against an LLM-annotated reference (not human gold) undermines measurement fidelity, especially for open-ended and FITB comparisons that drive several key metrics.
The construction of yes/no and true/false instances from base items is only briefly described; potential artifacts (e.g., unnatural or ambiguous negation constructions) could confound results.
DCS aggregates CFA and CPA within domains, implicitly mixing format and paraphrase axes with different sampling sizes; the interpretability and calibration of this composite could be clearer.


Experimental gaps or methodological issues
No human validation for either format conversions or paraphrases; LLM-based validation may share biases with the evaluated models and the judge, risking systematic error.
The paraphrase subset is relatively small (300 questions) and generated by a model included among those evaluated (Qwen2.5-14B), potentially introducing stylistic bias.
The largest model is evaluated on a reduced subset (500 questions) and in 4-bit quantization, complicating fair cross-scale comparisons and possibly underestimating best-case consistency.
It is unclear whether accuracy on open-ended/FITB uses the same equivalence judge; if so, judge noise affects not only consistency but also accuracy comparisons.


Clarity or presentation issues
Minor equation rendering artifacts (e.g., CPA definition) and some ambiguous phrasing around the accuracy evaluation protocol for free-form formats.
Claims such as “zero false positives” in the rule-based stage rely on LLM-labeled calibration and should be tempered.


Missing related work or comparisons
While OSQ-bench, RoParQ, and honesty/robustness suites are cited, a deeper discussion contrasting CONSISTBENCH’s metrics and design choices with large-scale paraphrase robustness studies (e.g., broader MCQ-only paraphrasing works) could better situate novelty.
Contamination concerns (e.g., MMLU/ARC exposure) are not analyzed; format inflation (MCQ) could be exacerbated by training-data familiarity.
sections.detailed_comments:
Technical soundness evaluation
The metric definitions are reasonable and align with the stated goals of decoupling consistency from accuracy; item-level agreement across pairs is the right unit of analysis. However, the measurement pipeline’s heaviest reliance on an LLM judge with modest agreement and LLM-derived calibration labels constitutes a primary validity threat. The constrained-pair analysis partially alleviates this for CFA but not for CPA or open/FITB accuracy.
The negation-specific findings are compelling and statistically robust, and they cohere with broader literature identifying negation as a key failure mode in QA and adversarial setups.
The design of yes/no and true/false transformations could benefit from more detailed specification and safeguards (e.g., controlled negation syntax, ambiguity screens, adversarial distractor validation).


Experimental evaluation assessment
The sample sizes for cross-format evaluation are adequate for 3.8B–14B models and partially adequate for 70B given CIs and clear disclosure. The paraphrase subset size (300 per type) is enough to magnify the negation effect, but likely underpowered to detect small differences among the five non-negation types—this is acknowledged and argued prudently.
Reporting of bootstrap CIs, pairwise agreement heatmaps, and family-specific scaling analyses is thorough. The consistency–accuracy decoupling analysis is conceptually important and well-illustrated by Δ = CFA − Accuracy.
Potential implementation fragility (format-specific extraction and parsing) is not stress-tested or audited with human checks; such errors could inflate inconsistency rates for free-form formats.


Comparison with related work (using the summaries provided)
Relative to OSQ-bench (Myrzakhan et al., 2024), this work moves beyond MCQ-to-open accuracy gaps by measuring item-level agreement across five formats and adding controlled paraphrase analysis with stratified types. It complements Lunardi et al. (2025) and RoParQ by broadening beyond MCQ-only paraphrase robustness and providing domain-stratified consistency diagnostics. It is in line with the trend of evaluating robustness/consistency under realistic perturbations seen in enterprise robustness suites, but with a sharper focus on format and paraphrase axes.
The finding that negation is uniquely destabilizing echoes adversarial QA analyses (e.g., AddSent negation confusion) and recent PRIN-style results highlighting discriminative negation failures. This convergence strengthens the paper’s central message about negation.


Discussion of broader impact and significance
The benchmark draws attention to a critical failure surface in deployed systems: models’ answers can change across benign format or phrasing shifts, with negation being especially risky. These insights are timely and valuable for evaluation designers, safety practitioners, and downstream users in high-stakes domains.
However, the current reliance on LLM judges and lack of human validation weakens its suitability as a de facto standard at this stage; a follow-up with human-validated ground truth would substantially elevate its impact.
The authors’ transparency about limitations is commendable and suggests a roadmap: human-verified equivalence sets, judge ensembles and calibration procedures, expanded model coverage (including proprietary), and broader task classes (reasoning/multistep).
sections.questions_for_authors:
How is accuracy computed for open-ended and fill-in-the-blank formats—do you use the same two-stage equivalence pipeline against gold answers? If so, can you report precision/recall of the judge relative to human labels specifically for accuracy evaluation (not just pairwise equivalence)?
Can you provide a small human-annotated audit (e.g., 200 items) for (a) format conversions (especially yes/no and true/false) and (b) paraphrase semantic equivalence, and re-report key metrics on that subset to quantify the effect of LLM-only validation?
For the negation paraphrase type—which also scored lowest in LLM-based paraphrase quality—how much of the observed fragility remains on a human-validated negation subset? Could you share a per-type error decomposition (semantic drift vs. model sensitivity)?
How were yes/no and true/false variants constructed to avoid pragmatic oddities or entailment ambiguities (e.g., scope of negation, presuppositions, world-knowledge defaults)? Do you have rule templates or heuristics you can share, and did you check for label leakage (e.g., overly lexicalized cues)?
Since Qwen2.5-14B generated paraphrases and was also evaluated, did you observe any stylistic bias that advantages Qwen models? Could you replicate paraphrase generation with a different backbone (e.g., Mistral/Llama) and compare CPA/PFI stability?
Can you include a judge ablation (e.g., stronger judges, majority voting across diverse models, or CoT-based judging) and report how CFA/CPA shift, particularly for open-involving pairs?
For DCS, why average CFA and CPA rather than report them separately per domain? Given unequal sample sizes and different noise profiles, can you show that the composite preserves rank orderings and effect sizes seen in its components?
Did you analyze potential training contamination effects (e.g., MMLU familiarity) as a contributor to format gaps (especially MCQ inflation)? Any evidence from out-of-domain subsets?
sections.overall_assessment:
Estimated Score:
                    6.3/10
                    (Calibrated to ICLR scale)
                
            This paper tackles an important and underexplored dimension of LLM evaluation: item-level consistency across formats and controlled paraphrase types. The combined design, metric suite, and analyses convey clear, practically significant findings—most notably, that consistency is well below ideal across formats, and negation paraphrases are uniquely destabilizing across models and domains. The writing is clear and limitations are squarely addressed.
However, the methodological foundation relies heavily on LLM-only validation and an LLM judge with modest agreement against an LLM-labeled reference. This poses a serious threat to measurement validity precisely in the regimes (open-ended/FITB) where the strongest claims are made. Although the constrained-pair analysis salvages the core message for cross-format CFA, the lack of human calibration for equivalence, format conversions, and paraphrases dampens confidence in absolute levels and some comparative claims. Additional concerns include the limited paraphrase sample size, potential bias from using Qwen2.5-14B to generate paraphrases, reduced 70B coverage, and incomplete clarity about the accuracy computation pipeline for free-form outputs.
Overall, CONSISTBENCH is a promising and useful direction with nontrivial insights that the community would benefit from. In its current form, it is close to being impactful but falls short of top-tier bar due to the evaluation pipeline’s dependence on uncalibrated LLM judgments. I encourage the authors to include a human-validated subset and a judge ablation, clarify construction protocols for yes/no and true/false, and strengthen open/FITB accuracy evaluation. With these revisions, the benchmark would be substantially more reliable and publishable at a top venue.

                    CONSISTBENCH: A Cross-Format, Cross-Phrasing Consistency Benchmark for Large Language Models
                    
                        
                            
                            ICLR
                        
                        
                            
                            Submitted: April 9, 2026
                        
                    
                

                
                    
                    
                        
                            Contents
                            SummaryStrengthsWeaknessesDetailed CommentsQuestionsOverall Assessment
                        
                    

                    
                    
                        
                            
                                
                                Summary
                            
                            This paper introduces CONSISTBENCH, a benchmark designed to evaluate LLM consistency along two orthogonal axes: cross-format (MCQ, open-ended, yes/no, true/false, fill-in-the-blank) and cross-phrasing (six paraphrase types) with an additional domain-stratified analysis across six knowledge areas. It proposes a suite of consistency metrics (CFA, CPA, PFI, FCAG, DCS) that decouple consistency from accuracy and reports empirical findings on six open-weight models (3.8B–70B), showing large cross-format inconsistency (CFA ~56–63%), pronounced fragility to negation-framing paraphrases, and sizable domain variation. The authors validate their pipeline with a two-stage answer-equivalence system (rule-based + LLM judge) and perform sensitivity analyses that show the core findings persist even when restricting to constrained formats that do not require an LLM judge.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Strengths
                            
                            
Technical novelty and innovation
The joint evaluation of cross-format and paraphrase-type-stratified consistency at the item level is new and practically relevant; prior work typically studies either format conversion or paraphrase robustness in isolation.
The metric suite (CFA, CPA, PFI, FCAG, DCS) is well-motivated to separate consistency from accuracy and enables a multi-faceted diagnosis.
The explicit typology of paraphrase categories, with controlled negation framing, reveals a sharp and actionable pattern (negation vs. non-negation) across models.


Experimental rigor and validation
The sensitivity analysis that separates constrained pairs (string-match only) from open-involving pairs helps bound the effect of LLM-judge errors and preserves the headline conclusion (CFA far from 100%).
Sound statistical reporting (bootstrap CIs, McNemar tests with Bonferroni corrections, ANOVA/Kruskal-Wallis for domain effects); consistency–accuracy decoupling analysis is clearly presented.
Scaling and domain breakdowns provide deeper insights beyond aggregate metrics.


Clarity of presentation
The paper is generally well-written, with clear definitions of metrics and transparent acknowledgment of limitations.
Figures and tables are informative and align with the major claims; the narrative emphasizes item-level agreement rather than only aggregate accuracy.


Significance of contributions
Consistency is central to reliable deployment; diagnosing cross-format and paraphrase sensitivity
